context: Beginning in verse number 1, the Bible says, "...that which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled of the word of life.

context: that which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled of the word of life
point_summary: John's opening declaration — "which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled" — directly counters Docetist denial of Christ's humanity by grounding the incarnation in apostolic eyewitness testimony.

point_summary: Confession of sin restores fellowship with God, as 1 John 1:9 promises that God is faithful and just to forgive and cleanse the believer who comes to Him broken and honest about their sin.

context: I want to go into chapter number 2
point_summary: First John 2 is introduced as the broader context for understanding sin in the believer's life, countering antinomianism by affirming that salvation does not license ungodly living.

context: if we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves. The truth is not in us
point_summary: Self-deception about sinlessness is exposed as a lie that makes God a liar, contrasted with the confession that brings His faithful forgiveness and cleansing.

context: if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness
point_summary: Confession of sin to God — not denial of it — is the believer's appointed path to forgiveness and cleansing, set against the antinomian lie that a saved person may live however he pleases.

context: If we say that we have not sinned, we make Him a liar. His word is not in us
point_summary: Claiming sinlessness makes God a liar, confirming that even believers will sin—a reality that makes confession, not denial, the only right response.

context: my little children, these things write I unto you that you sin not
point_summary: Written to believers, 1 John 2:1's command "that ye sin not" directly echoes Psalm 4:4, reinforcing that God's standard for His children is progressive victory over sin, not sinless perfection.

context: according to verse 8 and verse 10 of chapter 1
point_summary: Honest acknowledgment of sin—rather than self-deception—is the necessary precondition for receiving God's faithful forgiveness and cleansing, with 1 John 1:8 framing the reality that believers will sin.

context: according to verse 8 and verse 10 of chapter 1
point_summary: Denying sinlessness makes God a liar, but honest confession meets His faithfulness to forgive—establishing that believers will sin and need the advocacy of Christ.

context: We have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous
point_summary: Confession of sin, met with God's faithfulness and justice to forgive and cleanse, is the believer's recourse when they fall short of the standard "sin not."

context: if we say we have no sin that we're deceiving ourselves, we're making God a liar, His truth is not in us
point_summary: Denying personal sinfulness is self-deception that makes God a liar, since believers are not yet freed from the flesh and still require Christ's advocacy and atoning propitiation.

context: We have an advocate with the Father
point_summary: Unlike a worldly defense attorney who argues innocence despite guilt, Christ as advocate declares the believer guilty but fully covered—His atoning work, not our innocence, is the ground of our standing before the Father.

context: He is the propitiation for our sins, the mercy seat, the covering, and not for ours only, but also for the sins of the whole world
point_summary: Christ as advocate does not argue innocence but declares the guilty sinner covered—because He Himself is the propitiation, the mercy seat, that fully satisfies the Father's justice.

context: according to 1 John 1, verse 8 and verse 10
point_summary: Denying personal sin is self-deception that makes God a liar, as 1 John 1:8 and 10 are cited to rebuke any claim of sinlessness.

context: according to 1 John 1, verse 8 and verse 10
point_summary: Claiming to have no sin makes God a liar, as 1 John 1:10 is cited alongside verse 8 to confront any self-deception about one's sinfulness before a holy God.

context: the Bible says if I confess my sin
point_summary: Genuine confession means agreeing with God that sin is wickedness — not minimizing it as "not that big" — as the Hebrew concept behind "confess" implies saying the same thing God says about it.

context: greater is He that is in me that He that is in the world
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: What did John tell us in 1 John that we're to try the spirits?
point_summary: Believers must know Scripture well enough to test teachers against it, because false apostles are skilled at appearing to be what they are not.

context: greater is he that is in you than he that is in the world
point_summary: Greater is He that is in you than he that is in the world (1 John 4:4) grounds the believer's confidence that, despite Satan's relentless warfare against God's people, victory and overcoming are possible.

context: On the authority of God's Word, you're lying and you're making God a liar. 1 John 1
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 1 John chapter number one. Look at the verse with me if you will. 1 John chapter number one.
point_summary: Agreeing with God—aligning one's own assessment of sin with what God declares—is presented as essential preparation for revival, over against self-deception or cultural redefinition of sin.

context: if we confess our sins
point_summary: Confessing sin, drawn from the Greek meaning of *homologeo*, is defined here as agreeing with God—calling what He calls sin exactly what it is, without minimizing or comparing oneself to others.

context: at the end of this book, 1 John, it says my little children keep yourself from idols
point_summary: Closing 1 John with "keep yourselves from idols," the sermon defines an idol as anything placed before God's preeminence—a standard demanding confession and agreement with God for revival readiness.

context: If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just. Notice what it says, to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from what? From all unrighteousness
point_summary: Confessing sin, drawn from the Greek meaning of "confess," means agreeing with God about what sin actually is—calling it wicked and ungodly rather than minimizing or comparing it to others' offenses.

context: if you say that you have no sin, that you're a liar and you make God a liar
point_summary: Denying personal sin makes one a liar before God, but honest confession brings forgiveness and cleansing — a prerequisite the sermon frames as essential to revival readiness.

context: my little children, these things I write unto you, verse 1 of chapter 2, that ye sin not. And if any man sin, you're going to. You have. We have an advocate with the Father. Jesus Christ the righteous
point_summary: Even when believers sin, they have an advocate in Jesus Christ the righteous — a provision that makes repentance possible and revival readiness attainable.

context: When you walk in the light as He is in the light, man, have that fellowship, that communion, that abiding
point_summary: Walking in the light as God is in the light is presented as the pathway to genuine fellowship and communion with Him, framed as the "draw nigh to God" step in revival readiness.

context: If we ask anything according to his will, he heareth us. And if we know that he hear us, whatsoever we ask, we know that we have the petitions that we've desired of him
point_summary: Confident, answered prayer becomes possible when believers move beyond self-centered asking and align their petitions with God's will rather than their own limited perception.

context: he's the propitiation for our sins but not for ours only but for the sins of the whole world
point_summary: Christ's propitiation extends beyond the local congregation to the sins of the whole world, grounding the individual believer's assurance that God loves and is concerned for each person personally.

context: He that hath the Son hath life. He that rejects or hath not the Son of God hath not life
point_summary: Possession of eternal life is inseparable from receiving the Son, making faith in Christ the sole dividing line between life and its absence.

context: You read the book of 1 John, John talks about we're witnesses of these things and we're now telling you these things
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: If we confess our sins
point_summary: Confession of sin is the necessary gateway to restored fellowship with God, because unacknowledged offense—illustrated by Adam's blame-shifting—leaves that fellowship broken regardless of one's standing before God.

context: I want to walk in the light as he is in the light
point_summary: Walking in the light as God is in the light—the fellowship 1 John 1:7 describes—requires first acknowledging sin, since unconfessed offense leaves that fellowship broken.

context: he is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness
point_summary: Hiding from God and covering sin brings no profit; the wiser response is to fall on God's mercy, trusting His faithfulness to forgive and cleanse as promised.

context: He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness
point_summary: Forgiveness is available from God when we confess our sins, as He is faithful and just to forgive and cleanse from all unrighteousness—one of three needs David expressed in Psalm 25.

context: these things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God, that ye may know, that ye may know that you have eternal life
point_summary: Assurance of salvation is the stated purpose of 1 John 5:13—God wrote these things so believers may *know* they have eternal life, not merely hope or wonder.

context: greater is he that is in you than he that is in the world
point_summary: Though the devil is a real and active adversary, the indwelling Christ is greater than him, making the believer's enemy a defeated foe who cannot ultimately prevail.

context: Faith is the victory
point_summary: Overcoming by faith, as taught in 1 John 5, is the ground on which the promise to Smyrna rests — those who overcome shall not be hurt of the second death.

context: there are many anti-Christ and the spirit of anti-Christ is already at work
point_summary: John's warning that many antichrists are already at work grounds the call to be settled and grounded in the faith rather than swayed by false teaching or demands for open-mindedness.

context: love not the world, in first John two and verse 15, neither the things that are in the world
point_summary: Loving the present world, as illustrated by Demas's desertion of Paul, is the practical consequence John warns against when he commands believers not to love the world.

context: looking in the truth in first John
point_summary: Scripture's witness in 1 John that God created man for fellowship undergirds the sermon's claim that God foreknew the fall and predetermined redemption through Christ before creation.

context: greater is he that is in you than he that is in the world
point_summary: God's greater power over Satan—affirmed by John's declaration that "greater is he that is in you than he that is in the world"—grounds the certainty that Jesus's authoritative command over the demon must be obeyed.

context: We're familiar with 1 John 1 and verse number 9, if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.
point_summary: Confession as the proper result of self-examination means agreeing with God specifically about known sin—not vague "if I have sinned" prayers—trusting His faithfulness to forgive and cleanse.

context: Love not the world, neither the things that are in the world
point_summary: Loving the world fills our hands so completely that we cannot cleave to Christ or speak of Him to others.

context: it passeth away, amen? But he that doeth the will of the Father abideth forever
point_summary: The world's passing nature (1 John 2:17) is the reason full hands leave believers too preoccupied to speak of Jesus, since clinging to worldly things crowds out both devotion and witness.

context: 1 John 2
point_summary: Clinging to Christ requires empty hands — the world's passing nature (1 John 2) exposes how worldly attachments crowd out both devotion to Jesus and witness about him.

context: he is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness
point_summary: God's faithfulness to forgive and cleanse confessed sin (1 John 1:9) is set against the enemy's ongoing accusation, assuring believers that what God forgives, He no longer remembers.

context: first John chapter five in verse number nine, if we confess our sins, he is what? He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness
point_summary: Divine faithfulness to forgive and cleanse from all unrighteousness, grounded in 1 John 5:9, assures believers that God's promised forgetting of confessed sin is certain.

context: 1 John says that if you say that you have no sin, the truth is not in you and you make God a liar
point_summary: Denying personal sinfulness is not merely self-deception but makes God himself a liar, establishing the universal need for the pardon and mercy this sermon celebrates.

context: greater is he that
point_summary: God's creatorship over Satan establishes His supremacy, grounding the assurance of 1 John 4:4 that the Creator dwelling in believers is greater than the created adversary in the world.

context: In 1 John 2, the Bible would give us this truth
point_summary: The three-fold pattern of sin in Genesis 3:6—lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, and pride of life—is identified as the same course of sin catalogued in 1 John 2:15–17.

context: in 1 John 2 and verses 15-17, the Bible says, love not the world, neither the things that are in the world
point_summary: 1 John 2:15–17's triad of the lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, and pride of life is presented as the New Testament parallel that names and explains the precise sequence of desire Eve followed in Genesis 3:6.

context: We're going to be changed. We're going to be like Him
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: behold what manner of love the father hath bestowed upon us that we should be called the sons of God
point_summary: The Father's bestowal of love upon believers, making them sons of God, is cited as the greatest of all the analogies Scripture uses to define the relationship between God and his people.

context: In first John four and verse number 14, the Bible says this, and we have seen and do testify that the father sent the son to be the savior of the world.
point_summary: 1 John 4:14 is presented as the clearest biblical summary of Christmas, declaring that the Father's purpose in sending the Son was precisely and exclusively to be the Savior of the world.

reference_text: first John four and verse number 14
context: this declaration in first John four and verse number 14
point_summary: 1 John 4:14 is read as the clearest biblical summary of Christmas: the Father's purpose in sending the Son was precisely and exclusively to be the Savior of the world, meeting humanity's greatest and most universal need.

context: first John chapter four and verse number 14, that John says, and we have seen and do testify that the father sent the son to be the savior of the world
point_summary: The universal scope of Christ's saving mission — "savior of the world" — raises the personal question of how that cosmic salvation becomes one's own, answered through his sinless life, atoning death, and shed blood.

context: Walk in the light as he is in the light, 1 John
point_summary: Walking with God means enjoying genuine fellowship with him, a privilege made possible only when we walk in the light, since claiming fellowship while walking in darkness is a lie.

context: If we say that we have fellowship with him, but we walk not in the light, we lie. The truth's not in us
point_summary: Walking with God requires walking in the light, since claiming fellowship with him while walking in darkness is a lie that exposes the absence of true fellowship.

context: 1 John dealing there much about the idea of fellowship
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: if you love the world, he says that the love of God is not in you
point_summary: Loving the world and loving God are mutually exclusive, and so God's people are called to separation and holiness rather than conformity to the world system.

context: Love not the world
point_summary: Loving the world and loving God are mutually exclusive, as John 15:19 confirms: the world's approval of a believer signals spiritual compromise, not faithfulness.

context: In 1 John 3, notice over there in the text of Scripture
point_summary: Cain's fratricide is invoked in 1 John 3 as the negative example against which the apostle John defines the Christian obligation to love the brethren.

context: in 1 John 3, in verse 11. for this is the message that you've heard from the beginning, that we should love one another.
point_summary: John's use of Cain as a negative example in 1 John 3:11–12 grounds the New Testament command to love one another in the oldest recorded failure of brotherly love, showing that hatred rooted in evil works leads to violence against the righteous.

context: Notice this, not as Cain, who was of that wicked one and slew his brother. And wherefore slew he him? Because his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous.
point_summary: John's use of Cain as a negative example illustrates that hatred of the righteous—rooted in evil works—is the antithesis of the love-one-another command running throughout John's writings.

context: Notice what the text of Scripture teaches us in verse number 13.
point_summary: Hatred of the righteous by the world is not surprising, because Cain's murder of Abel establishes the pattern: those who walk with God should expect the world's hostility.

context: that's the illustration that John uses in 1 John 3
point_summary: John's use of Cain and Abel in 1 John 3 reaches back to the very beginning of human history to establish a paradigm for understanding the hatred the world directs toward the righteous.

context: if we confess our sins he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness
point_summary: Confession (the Greek *homologeō*, "to say the same thing") means agreeing with God that sin is sin rather than excusing or rationalizing it, with the promise of forgiveness and cleansing following that honest acknowledgment.

context: if we hate our brother
point_summary: Hating a brother, like Cain's murder, constitutes a violation of God's holy commands that the Holy Spirit confronts through conviction.

context: in 1 John chapter 1, the Bible talks about having fellowship with God
point_summary: Fellowship with God, promised in 1 John 1, is distinguished here from relationship: sin does not sever the believer's standing as God's child, but it does hinder communion with Him.

context: first John two and verse number six, and the Bible says, he that sayeth he abideth in him ought himself also so to walk even as he walked
point_summary: Abiding in Christ carries an obligatory claim: those who profess to abide in Him must study the Gospels to discover concretely how Christ walked, then conform their lives to that pattern.

context: first John two, six, really requires us as students of the word
point_summary: Abiding in Christ carries a behavioral obligation—claiming that abiding relationship requires the believer to investigate the Gospels and replicate the specific manner in which Christ himself walked during his earthly ministry.

context: my little children, keep yourselves from idols
point_summary: Idolatry's enduring danger is underscored by John's closing charge—"keep yourselves from idols"—as the sermon connects Old Testament Israel's repeated failure to the varied but real forms idolatry takes today.

context: love not the world, neither the things that are in the world
point_summary: Separation from the world is not merely negative avoidance but a positive turning *to* God, grounded in 1 John 2:15's command that love of the world and love of God are mutually exclusive.

context: the things of the world, they're going to pass away, but he that doeth the will of God abideth forever
point_summary: Separation from the world is not merely a negative command but a positive reorientation, since the world passes away while the one who does God's will abides forever.

context: we look at the book of First John and we understand that we are victorious
point_summary: Believers are overcomers not by their own strength but because, as 1 John 5:4 declares, whatever is born of God overcomes the world through faith.

context: greater is he that is in us than he that is in the world
point_summary: The believer's identity as an overcomer rests not on personal strength but on the indwelling God who is greater than the world's opposing force.

reference_text: First John chapter five in verse number four
context: for whatsoever is born of God over cometh the world and this is the victory that overcometh the world even our faith
point_summary: Victory over the world belongs to all who are born of God through faith, grounding the elders' seated, overcoming position in heaven in the promise of 1 John 5:4.

context: In 1 John 3 and verse number 8
point_summary: Christ's manifestation in the flesh is declared in 1 John 3:8 as purposefully aimed at destroying the devil's works, confirming the cross and resurrection as God's decisive victory over Satan.
